title: CC Exercises — Backoffice & CMS
description: Hands-on exercises covering Backoffice customisation, CMS page/component creation, custom CMS components, and SmartEdit personalisation.

sections.title: Exercise 1 — Customise the Backoffice Product Editor
sections.content:
Objective

Modify the Backoffice product editor to show a custom attribute, reorganise tabs, and restrict field visibility by role.

Steps

  1. If you haven't already, add a custom attribute to the Product type in items.xml — for example, warrantyMonths (Integer). Run system update.
  2. Open the product editor in Backoffice and verify the attribute does NOT appear yet.
  3. In your extension's backoffice-config.xml, add a field configuration for warrantyMonths:
    • Place it in the "General" tab, after the description field.
    • Set a label: "Warranty (months)".
    • Set it as editable.
  4. Create a new tab called "Logistics" in the product editor via backoffice-config.xml. Move the weight and unit fields to this tab.
  5. Restart the server (or reset Backoffice in dev mode). Verify the new field and tab appear.
  6. Configure Access Rights in Backoffice: make the warrantyMonths field read-only for the backofficecmsmanagergroup role (CMS managers shouldn't edit product warranty data). Verify by switching user roles.

Validation

The warranty field appears in the correct tab. The Logistics tab contains the moved fields. CMS managers see the field as read-only while product managers can edit it.

sections.title: Exercise 2 — Build a CMS Page with Custom Components
sections.content:
Objective

Create a complete CMS page from scratch: page template, content page, content slots, standard components, and a custom CMS component type.

Steps

Part A — Page Structure (via ImpEx)

  1. Create a PageTemplate called TrainingLandingPageTemplate with the following content slots: HeaderSlot, HeroBannerSlot, BodyContentSlot, FooterSlot.
  2. Create a ContentPage called TrainingLandingPage using this template. Set the label to /training and provide a title, description, and keywords for SEO.
  3. Create ContentSlot instances for each slot position and assign them to the page via ContentSlotForPage relations.
  4. Add standard components to the slots: a BannerComponent in HeroBannerSlot (with an image and headline), a CMSParagraphComponent in BodyContentSlot (with HTML rich text).

Part B — Custom CMS Component

  1. In items.xml, create a custom type FeatureHighlightComponent extending SimpleCMSComponent. Add attributes: icon (String — emoji or icon class), title (localised String), description (localised String), linkUrl (String).
  2. Run system update.
  3. Add the new type to backoffice-config.xml so it's editable in Backoffice CMS.
  4. Register it in a component type group so it appears in the "Add Component" dropdown.
  5. Via ImpEx, create 3 instances of FeatureHighlightComponent and add them to the BodyContentSlot.

Validation

Navigate to /training on the storefront (or check via OCC CMS API if using Spartacus). Verify the page renders with the hero banner and paragraph. The custom components will need a frontend renderer to display — verify they exist in the CMS API response even if not yet rendered visually.

sections.title: Exercise 3 — SmartEdit Content Editing & Versioning
sections.content:
Objective

Use SmartEdit to visually edit a page, create a page version, make changes, and then roll back to the saved version.

Prerequisites

Access to a SmartEdit-enabled storefront (accelerator or Spartacus with @spartacus/smartedit configured).

Steps

  1. Open SmartEdit by navigating to the /smartedit URL.
  2. Navigate to the homepage (or any content page with editable components).
  3. Create a Page Version: click the versioning icon in the toolbar, name it "Before Changes", and save.
  4. Edit a BannerComponent: click on a banner, modify the headline text and swap the image. Save.
  5. Add a new component: find an empty slot (or a slot with space), click the "Add Component" button, and add a CMSParagraphComponent with some promotional text.
  6. Reorder components: drag the paragraph component above the banner within the same slot. Verify the order changed in the preview.
  7. Now restore the page version: go to the versioning panel, select "Before Changes", and restore it. Verify all your changes are reverted — the original banner text, image, and component order are back.

Bonus Challenge

If the approval workflow is enabled: make a change and submit it for approval. Log in as a reviewer and approve/reject the change. Verify the workflow status transitions.

Validation

The page version captures the complete state. Restoring it reverts all changes. The editing experience is WYSIWYG — what you see in SmartEdit matches what customers see after sync.

sections.title: Exercise 4 — SmartEdit Personalisation
sections.content:
Objective

Create a personalised experience in SmartEdit where different customer segments see different content in the same slot.

Steps

  1. In SmartEdit, navigate to a page with a prominent content slot (e.g. the homepage hero area).
  2. Open the Personalisation panel (Combined View / Target Groups).
  3. Create or identify two target groups:
    • VIPCustomers — customers belonging to a specific customer group (e.g. a loyalty tier group you created).
    • NewVisitors — customers who are not logged in (anonymous users).
  4. Create a variation for VIPCustomers: in the hero slot, replace the default banner with a "Welcome Back, VIP!" banner featuring exclusive offers.
  5. Create a variation for NewVisitors: in the hero slot, show a "New to our store? Get 10% off your first order!" banner.
  6. Leave the default experience with the standard banner for all other logged-in users.
  7. Use SmartEdit's variation switcher to preview each experience: toggle between Default, VIPCustomers, and NewVisitors and verify each shows the correct banner.
  8. Sync the page to Online.

Validation

Preview all three experiences in SmartEdit and verify the correct content shows for each segment. If possible, test on the live storefront: visit as anonymous (NewVisitors banner), log in as a regular customer (default banner), and log in as a VIP customer (VIP banner). The personalisation engine selects the right variation at runtime based on customer data.
